summary.brief: RIVES, M.D. v. FARRIS C/W 81052. Medical malpractice case involving surgery general.

evidence_index.text: Respondent Titina Farris suffered from back pain with pain and burning in her feet. She was diagnosed with uncontrolled diabetes causing neuropathy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Rives diagnosed Farris with a hernia, which he surgically repaired on two occasions, first in 2014 and second in 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the second surgery, Rives noticed that part of Ferris's colon was stuck in the mesh from the 2014 surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Rives freed the colon from the mesh; however, he caused two small holes in the colon, which he repaired with a stapling device.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Farris had several problems following the 2015 surgery, including sepsis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Although a CT scan on July 5 and an x-ray on July 12 showed no signs of a leak in Farris's colon, a CT scan on July 15 showed a leak, which another surgeon corrected.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Farris's sepsis continued, and she eventually developed drop foot in both feet, hindering her ability to walk unassisted.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court abused its discretion by admitting evidence of another medical malpractice case against appellant Barry James Rives, M.D.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We reverse the judgment, vacate the attorney fees and costs order, and remand for a new trial.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The plain language of our jurisdictional rules confirms that appellants are not required to file a motion for a new trial in district court to preserve their ability to request a new trial on appeal.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented with swelling in the upper abdomen. Previously diagnosed with uncontrolled diabetes causing neuropathy, experiencing back pain with pain and burning in her feet.

requestables.name: CT Scan
requestables.description: CT scan to check for leaks in the colon

reveal.result_summary: CT scan on July 5 and x-ray on July 12 showed no signs of a leak; CT scan on July 15 showed a leak
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicated a leak in the colon post-surgery
reveal.decision_impact: Prompted surgical intervention to correct the leak

requestables.name: X-ray
requestables.description: X-ray to check for complications post-surgery

reveal.result_summary: No signs of a leak in the colon

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with swelling in the upper abdomen

timeline_phases.name: Second Hernia Repair Surgery
timeline_phases.description: Surgery to repair hernia and address complications from previous surgery

key_events.event: Colon freed from mesh, two holes repaired

timeline_phases.name: Postoperative Complications
timeline_phases.description: Patient develops sepsis and other complications

key_events.event: CT scan shows leak in colon

decision_points.clinical_context: During the second hernia repair surgery
decision_points.prompt: How should the surgeon handle the colon stuck in the mesh?

options.description: Carefully free the colon and inspect for any damage
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to ensure no damage occurs during freeing of the colon

options.description: Free the colon and repair any damage with a stapling device
options.clinical_reasoning: Risk of causing further damage or not adequately repairing the colon

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant freed the colon and repaired two small holes with a stapling device

expected_action_court.description: Careful inspection and management to prevent damage
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony on standard surgical practices

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Caused two small holes in the colon during the procedure
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Careful inspection and management to prevent damage
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed sepsis and drop foot in both feet

key_findings: District court abused discretion by admitting irrelevant evidence
Case remanded for a new trial

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Admissibility of evidence, not clinical decision

factual_timeline.event: Patient diagnosed with hernia and first surgery performed
CT scan shows leak in colon
Patient presents with back pain and burning in feet

tests_performed.result: No signs of a leak in the colon
No signs of a leak in the colon

allegations.allegation: Failure to properly manage the colon during surgery
Failure to properly manage surgical complications

court_findings.legal_significance: Standard of care not met during surgery

court_findings.finding: District court abused discretion by admitting irrelevant evidence
court_findings.legal_significance: Case remanded for a new trial
